title: AI boom lifts risk to grid security
full_text:
Major electricity transmission companies including Transgrid, Ausnet and Vicgrid warn the rush to install major data centres has hiked security risks to Australia’s power grid, as authorities scramble to limit the threat of blackouts.
Data centres are expected to more than double their power needs by 2030, driven by an artificial-intelligence boom, with Treasurer Jim Chalmers betting on a bulging pipeline of data centre projects to carry business investment in the next few years.
However, experts are concerned that dozens of data centres face being knocked out simultaneously, as a wave of new power generation tests the technical limits of the electricity system, and jacks up the risk of supply shortages or even blackouts.
Transgrid outlined concern over emerging system security risks from co-located data centres simultaneously switching to on-site generation, particularly in western Sydney where up to 8 gigawatts of power generation for facilities have been proposed.
“Allowing connection applicants to keep using the existing access standards during the transition would allow large data centre projects in western Sydney to proceed without adequate technical requirements, creating a material power-system security risk,” Transgrid said in a submission to the Australian Energy Market Commission.
Most large data centres rely on power electronics similar to those used in solar panel and battery systems. During grid faults, such as voltage dips, they can rapidly reduce demand or disconnect entirely. If multiple facilities react at the same time, the sudden loss of demand can destabilise the power system and potentially trigger cascading outages.
“We believe that the disconnection of gigawatts of load following a non-credible contingency event in western Sydney may lead to cascading outages or major supply disruptions,” Transgrid said.
The AEMC has proposed new technical standards that would require large data centres to stay connected during disturbances on the electricity grid and respond to instability, reflecting concerns the facilities behave more like power generators than traditional energy users.
VicGrid warned that AI electricity needs were characterised by abrupt power swings. “If these fluctuations are passed directly to the grid without any explicit control, they can create sudden frequency deviations, local voltage variations, and sharp reactive power swings,” the company said.
The proposed AEMC rules would introduce new “ride-through” requirements, forcing large electricity users to stay connected during voltage and frequency disturbances and quickly restore their power consumption once conditions stabilised.
Snowy Hydro said by requiring data centres to have “ride-through” capabilities, the rule will reduce the risk of cascading outages and system black events.
“This protects physical generating assets from sudden, damaging system-wide failures,” Snowy said in a submission.
Forecasts suggest if all proposed projects proceed, demand linked to data centres could reach as much as 34 terawatt hours – roughly equivalent to the annual electricity consumption of about six million Australian homes, and exceeding central forecasts and what would materially reshape the demand outlook.
Elon Musk’s Tesla expressed fears over any moves being rushed through without proper process.
“Power systems are designed around loads whose power variations occur predominantly below ~1 Hz. In contrast, data centres can exhibit power variations with frequency content well above 10 Hz. This introduces new and largely uncharted stability challenges, requiring extensive analysis and study to properly address,” Tesla said.
Data Centres Australia, the industry group representing the sector, said a six-months timeframe was not enough time for equipment manufacturers to develop the necessary models to comply.
“The cumulative effect across the sector’s pipeline could delay Australia’s data centre build-out by 6–12 months or more, with consequences well beyond the operators themselves, given the role of this infrastructure in cloud, AI, government, financial and household services. It will also potentially limit Australia’s ability to attract global investment in what is a global race to build AI infrastructure,” the group warned.
AusNet pointed to the loss of 1500MW of voltage-sensitive data centre demand in the US in July 2024, noting it had the potential to cause a much larger frequency deviation in Australia’s national electricity market.
Energy Minister Chris Bowen and Industry Minister Tim Ayres laid out expectations for data centres in March, with operators required to underwrite new renewable power supply and pay their full share of new grid connectivity so costs are not passed to consumers or businesses.
States, with the exception of Queensland, then approved the plan at an energy ministers meeting last Friday, while the national rule-maker is also drafting new grid rules for industry.
Airtrunk estimates for every additional gigawatt of data centre demand, Australia will likely need about 3–4 gigawatts of new renewable generation capacity to support 24/7 operations.
Under the current framework, electricity market rules do not clearly distinguish between traditional industrial facilities and large technology-driven loads such as data centres. In practice, network operators and the market operator have relied on guidelines that treat loads of 5MW or more as “large”, but those thresholds sit outside the formal rule book and have been applied inconsistently. The draft rule would define large facilities as those above 30MW, and introduce standards requiring them to manage impact on grid stability.
summary: Major electricity companies warn that the rapid growth of data centres, driven by AI, is increasing security risks to Australia's power grid, potentially leading to blackouts. New rules are being proposed to ensure these data centres maintain stability during power disturbances.
author: Perry Williams, Chief Business Corr
heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: Data centres' power needs could double by 2030.
Transgrid highlights risks from simultaneous power generation by data centres.
Proposed AEMC rules require data centres to stay connected during grid disturbances.
Forecasts suggest data centres could demand 34 terawatt hours of electricity.
Industry group warns new rules could delay data centre projects by 6-12 months.
ticker_line: Data centres raise power grid security concerns
